--- Page 1 ---
VARIATIONAL TRAJECTORY OPTIMIZATION OF ANISOTROPIC
DIFFUSION SCHEDULES
Pengxi Liu‚ąó
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering
Duke University
pengxi.liu@duke.edu
Zeyu Michael Li‚ąó
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering
Duke University
zeyu.li030@duke.edu
Xiang Cheng
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering
Duke University
xiang.cheng@duke.edu
ABSTRACT
We introduce a variational framework for diffusion models with anisotropic noise schedules pa-
rameterized by a matrix-valued path Mt(őł) that allocates noise across subspaces. Central to our
framework is a trajectory-level objective that jointly trains the score network and learns Mt(őł), which
encompasses general parameterization classes of matrix-valued noise schedules. We further derive an
estimator for ‚ąāőł‚ąálog pt that enables efficient optimization of the Mt(őł) schedule. For inference, we
develop an efficiently-implementable reverse-ODE solver that is an anisotropic generalization of the
second-order Heun discretization algorithm. Across CIFAR-10, AFHQv2, FFHQ, and ImageNet-64,
our method consistently improves upon the baseline EDM model in all NFE regimes. Code is
available at https://github.com/lizeyu090312/anisotropic-diffusion-paper.
Keywords Diffusion Model ¬∑ Anisotropic Diffusion
1
Introduction
Diffusion models generate samples by reversing a gradual Gaussian noising process. Starting from data x0 ‚ąľp0, a
forward process produces a family of noisy variables (xt)t‚Č•0 with density pt that smoothly interpolates between the
data distribution and a tractable Gaussian. A neural score model (parameters Ōē) is trained to approximate the score
‚ąáx log pt(x); given this score, sampling integrates a reverse-time SDE/ODE that transports pT back to p0 [1, 2, 3].
Nearly all modern diffusion pipelines assume isotropic forward noise, meaning that at time t, the covariance of total
injected Gaussian noise is a scalar multiple of identity.
Matrix-valued (anisotropic) schedules. In this paper, we replace the scalar noise level by a matrix-valued covariance
trajectory Mt and study how to learn it. Concretely, in the standard variance-exploding (VE) / Brownian formulation,
the isotropic process adds noise with covariance tI,
x0 ‚ąľp0,
dxt = dBt ‚áĒ
dxt = ‚ąí1
2‚ąálog pt(xt)dt,
(1)
where Bt is standard Brownian motion and ‚ąódenotes convolution. We generalize this to anisotropic diffusion by instead
considering a forward diffusion driven by a matrix-valued diffusion coefficient:
x0 ‚ąľp0,
dxt = (‚ąātMt)1/2dBt ‚áĒ
dxt = ‚ąí1
2‚ąātMt‚ąálog pt(xt)dt,
(2)
where Mt(őł) is a matrix-valued noise-schedule, with arbitrary parameterization őł, satisfying
M0 = 0,
‚ąātMt ‚ČĽ0
for all t.
(3)
‚ąóEqual contribution.
arXiv:2602.19512v1  [cs.LG]  23 Feb 2026


--- Page 2 ---
Isotropic 
Denoising
Anisotropic 
Denoising
ūĚúé2 ūĚĎ°:
ūĚĎ°:
ūĚĎĒ1 ūĚĎ°:
ūĚĎĒ2 ūĚĎ°:
5600             4800            4000            3200             2400            1600             800         
5600             4800            4000            3200             2400            1600             800         
4900             3600            2500            1600              900              400              100         
5600             4800            4000            3200             2400            1600             800         
(6400,6400)
Figure 1: Illustration of isotropic vs. anisotropic denoising. Top: a standard isotropic sampler denoises all directions
uniformly. Bottom: an anisotropic sampler with two DCT subspaces, V1 (low frequency) and V2 (high frequency)
(Section 6). Columns show intermediate reconstructions as t decreases. The plot (right) displays learned subspace
schedules g1(t) and g2(t); the former is denoised more aggressively, so low-frequency structure from V1 emerges
earlier, while high-frequency details from V2 emerge later. Illustration only: in practice anisotropic and isotropic
reconstruct different images, and the gap between g1 and g2 is typically smaller (see Fig. 2‚Äď5).
The power of anisotropy. Replacing a scalar schedule by a matrix path allows noise (and thus denoising effort)
to be allocated differently across directions and time, better matching data geometry. Natural images concentrate
energy in low spatial frequencies [4]; latent diffusion effectively separates coarse structure from fine detail [5]; video
models benefit from temporally structured priors or decomposed noise [6, 7]; and multi-resolution generation often
proceeds coarse-to-fine [8]. Figure 1 illustrates one such effect: low-frequency content can be denoised earlier than
high-frequency details when the schedule is allowed to differ across subspaces.
A variational formulation of matrix trajectory is necessary. The flexibility of Mt comes with a price: the design
space is enormous‚ÄĒinstead of choosing a single scalar function, we must choose a trajectory in the cone of PSD
matrices. In the isotropic VE case, the family of marginals is essentially one-dimensional: pt = p0 ‚ąóN(0, ŌÉ(t)2I)
depends on t only through the scalar variance ŌÉ(t)2, so changing the schedule mainly reparameterizes time along
the same curve of distributions. In contrast, different anisotropic paths Mt(őł) generally produce genuinely different
marginals pt(¬∑; őł) (even up to time reparameterization), and hence different score targets and reverse-time dynamics.
This makes hand-crafted anisotropy brittle and motivates a principled, data-driven approach.
1.1
Main contributions
We develop a variational framework for learning anisotropic diffusion trajectories Mt(őł) jointly with a score model
(parameters Ōē), rather than prescribing Mt by hand. We summarize our technical and empirical contributions below
(1) A general variational framework for learning anisotropic diffusion models.
1. We introduce a variational framework for anisotropic diffusion with matrix-valued noise path Mt(őł). Our framework
is fully general and supports broad classes of Mt(őł), including interpretable frequency-band schedules and data-
adaptive (class-conditional) PCA schedules (Section 6).
2. We propose a trajectory-level score-matching objective L(őł, Ōē) (Section 3) that jointly trains the score network and
learns Mt(őł) by minimizing mismatch between ideal and learned denoising dynamics along the reverse trajectory
(with a pathwise change-of-measure interpretation).
3. We derive anisotropic reverse-ODE solvers by generalizing Euler and second-order Heun updates to matrix trajecto-
ries (Section 5), yielding closed-form steps expressed through increments of M 1/2
t
and implementable efficiently
via structured matrix operations.
(2) An efficient schedule-gradient estimator for matrix-valued trajectories.
Optimizing Mt(őł) is challenging
because changing őł changes the entire forward family pt(¬∑; őł), and thus requires ‚ąāőł‚ąálog pt(x; őł), which is not
available from a network trained at a single őł. We derive a plug-in estimator of ‚ąāőł‚ąálog pt(x; őł) using only higher-
2


--- Page 3 ---
order x-directional derivatives of the network, implementable in three backward passes and independent of dim(őł)
(Theorem 4.1). We further introduce a flow parameterization that stabilizes scale across noise levels and reduces
estimator variance (Section 4.2).
(3) Empirical validation of anisotropic trajectory learning.
Across CIFAR-10, AFHQv2, FFHQ, and ImageNet-64
(Section 7), learned anisotropic trajectories are competitive with (and often improve upon) the EDM baseline under the
standard evaluation protocol. The gains persist across solver budgets and schedule families, supporting the view that
learning the matrix trajectory improves score-matching and the resulting generative model.
1.2
Related Work
Structured noising and anisotropy.
Prior work introduces non-isotropic structure via edge-aware perturbations
[9], correlated/blue-noise masks [10], subspace/frequency restrictions [11], and temporally structured priors for video
[7, 6, 12]. In contrast, we learn a global, monotone PSD matrix trajectory Mt(őł) (e.g., DCT/PCA-parameterized)
and optimize it jointly with the score network via a trajectory loss, allocating noise (and denoising effort) across
directions/subspaces.
ELBO views and schedule learning.
VDM [13] and the ELBO reinterpretation of common diffusion losses [14]
relate scalar schedule learning to reweighting noise levels. MuLAN [15] learns (possibly input-conditional) per-pixel
noise rates to tighten an ELBO for density estimation. Our formulation differs in that (i) Mt(őł) is anisotropic (reweights
directions, not just levels), (ii) we derive a practical schedule-gradient estimator that accounts for the schedule-dependent
score target, and (iii) we optimize the schedule to improve sample quality, yielding consistent FID gains.
2
Preliminaries
2.1
Anisotropic Diffusion: Process, Score, and Parameterizations
Recall the anisotropic diffusion process in (2). Let Mt(őł) denote the noise covariance at time t, parameterized by őł. pt
as defined in (2) has score given by
‚ąálog pt(x; őł) = M ‚ąí1
t
(őł)Ex0|xt=x [x0 ‚ąíxt] ,
(4)
where (x0, xt) are defined by the joint distribution x0 ‚ąľp0 and xt = x0 + N(0, Mt). We provide a short proof in
Lemma A.1 in Appendix A. In case of time-uniform isotropic diffusion (i.e. standard Brownian Motion), Mt(őł) = tI,
and the formula in (4) reduces to the standard score expression.
We parameterize a neural network net(x, t, Ōē) to approximate the score, we also define flow, a transformation of net
whose norm is approximately time-invariant:
net(x, t, Ōē) ‚Čą‚ąálog pt(x; őł)
(5)
flow(x, t, Ōē) := M 1/2
t
net(x, t, Ōē)
(6)
Continuous and discrete reverse ODE for anisotropic denoising.
Recall the forward ODE dynamics as defined in
(2): dxt = ‚ąí1
2‚ąātMt‚ąálog pt(xt)dt. Given net in (5), the learned denoising ODE is given by the time-reversal of
d¬Įxt = ‚ąí1
2‚ąātMt(őł)net(¬Įxt.t, Ōē)dt,
(7)
Variance-preserving anisotropic diffusion.
It is often more useful to consider the variance preserving anisotropic
diffusion, which is simply a time-dependent linear-transformation of (2). Define
xV P
t
:= (I + Mt(őł))‚ąí1/2xt.
(8)
The choice of I above is based on the assumption that Cov(x0) ‚ČąI, which implies that Cov(xV P
t
) ‚ČąI for all t.
Combining (8) with (2) gives the following dynamic:
dxV P
t
= v(xt, t; őł)dt,
where
v(x, t; őł) := ‚ąí1
2(I + Mt(őł))‚ąí1/2‚ąātMt(őł)‚ąálog pt(x; őł) ‚ąí1
2(I + Mt(őł))‚ąí3/2‚ąātMt(őł)x,
(9)
3

where v(x, t; őł) is the ideal velocity field. Analogously, we define the learned velocity field
d¬ĮxV P
t
= ¬Įv(xt, t; őł)dt,
where
¬Įv(x, t; őł, Ōē) := ‚ąí1
2(I + Mt(őł))‚ąí1/2‚ąātMt(őł)net(x, t, Ōē) ‚ąí1
2(I + Mt(őł))‚ąí3/2‚ąātMt(őł)x,
(10)
3
Trajectory-Level Score Matching Loss
This section introduces our central training objective (11), which enables us to jointly learn a matrix-valued noise path
{Mt(őł)}t‚ąą[0,T ] together with a score network net(x, t, Ōē). At a high level, the loss measures a trajectory-weighted
score error induced by the schedule Mt(őł), and it is designed so that (for any fixed őł) the optimal network recovers the
true score (Lemma 3.1).
3.1
Defining the Trajectory Loss
Loss definition.
We define our trajectory-level training objective as
L(őł, Ōē) := Et‚ąľUnif[0,T ], x0‚ąľp0, ŌĶ‚ąľN(0,I)
Wt(őł)

Mt(őł)1/2net

x0 + Mt(őł)1/2ŌĶ, t, Ōē

+ ŌĶ

2
2

where
Wt(őł) := (I + Mt(őł))‚ąí1/2(‚ąātMt(őł))Mt(őł)1/2.
(11)
In words: we draw a noise level t, perturb data by xt := x0 + Mt(őł)1/2ŌĶ, evaluate the score network at (xt, t), and
penalize its deviation from the noise target ‚ąíŌĶ, under a matrix-valued weight Wt(őł) determined by the trajectory
Mt(őł). Since xt = x0 + Mt(őł)1/2ŌĶ, we have Mt(őł)‚ąí1/2ŌĶ = ‚ąíMt(őł)‚ąí1(x0 ‚ąíxt), so the network term inside (11) is
trained to match the conditional denoising direction. The operator Wt(őł) serves as a matrix-valued weighting schedule
that can emphasize different subspaces at different times, and it is exactly this dependence on Mt(őł) that allows us to
optimize the trajectory őł.
Equivalence of loss to velocity mismatch norm.
Recall from Section 2 that under the variance-preserving (VP)
reparameterization (8), the ideal and learned VP velocity fields are v(¬∑, t; őł) and ¬Įv(¬∑, t; őł, Ōē) as defined in (9) and (10).
A natural trajectory-level principle is to choose (őł, Ōē) so that the learned denoising dynamics match the ideal ones along
the reverse trajectory. The continuous-time form of this principle is the path integral
Z T
0
Ex0‚ąľp0, ŌĶ‚ąľN(0,I)
h
‚ą•¬Įv(xt, t; őł, Ōē) ‚ąíňúv(xt, x0, t; őł)‚ą•2
2
i
dt,
xt = x0 + Mt(őł)1/2ŌĶ,
(12)
where ňúv(¬∑, x0, t; őł) is obtained from the ideal velocity v(¬∑, t; őł) by replacing the intractable score ‚ąálog pt(¬∑; őł) with the
single-sample proxy Mt(őł)‚ąí1(x0 ‚ąíxt) (see the score identity (4)):
ňúv(xt, x0, t; őł) := ‚ąí1
2(I + Mt(őł))‚ąí1/2‚ąātMt(őł) Mt(őł)‚ąí1(x0 ‚ąíxt) ‚ąí1
2(I + Mt(őł))‚ąí3/2‚ąātMt(őł) xt.
(13)
Expanding ¬Įv and ňúv, the common contraction term ‚ąí1
2(I + Mt)‚ąí3/2‚ąātMt xt cancels, and (12) reduces to the compact
matrix-weighted score-matching form (11) (up to an overall constant factor and the standard Monte Carlo approximation
of the time integral by sampling t).
3.2
Key Properties and Interpretation
Exact score at optimality.
As standard in the literature [16, 2], we show that for any fixed schedule Mt(őł) satisfying
(3), the optimal network for L(őł, Ōē) recovers the true score in the infinite-data/infinite-capacity limit:
Lemma 3.1 (Exact score at optimality). Fix any schedule Mt(őł) satisfying (3). In the limit of infinite data and model
capacity, the minimizer Ōē‚ąó(őł) = arg minŌē L(őł, Ōē) satisfies
net(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) = ‚ąáx log pt(x; őł).
(14)
We defer the proof of Lemma 3.1 to Appendix B.1.
Connection to path-wise change-of-measure (Girsanov).
The objective (12) is a path-wise integral of a velocity
mismatch, aligning with the classical change-of-measure control suggested by Girsanov‚Äôs theorem. Concretely, for
two processes dxt = v(xt, t) dt + dBt and d¬Įxt = ¬Įv(¬Įxt, t) dt + dBt, the path-space KL is bounded by a constant times
R T
0 E

‚ą•¬Įv(xt, t) ‚ąív(xt, t)‚ą•2
2

dt (under standard regularity, e.g. Novikov‚Äôs condition). Related bounds have been used
to control reverse SDE discretization error [17]. Our loss can be viewed as a tractable surrogate in this spirit: it replaces
the intractable score-dependent v with the proxy velocity ňúv in (13).
4


--- Page 5 ---
Consistency check: isotropic trajectory loss reduces to weighted score matching.
When Mt(őł) = g(t; őł)I for a
scalar function g, the weighting operator Wt(őł) becomes the scalar function
‚ąātg(t;őł)
‚ąö
1+g(t;őł). In Lemma A.2 (Appendix A),
we formally prove the equivalence to a standard weighted score-matching objective. Thus, in the isotropic case,
choosing g(t; őł) is essentially choosing a weighting over noise levels. Our main focus, however, is that (11) enables
optimization over matrix-valued trajectories.
4
Optimization of the Trajectory Loss
This section explains how we learn the matrix-valued trajectory Mt(őł). We first emphasize the subtle but crucial
relationship between őł and the learned network: the parametric model net(x, t, Ōē) has no explicit dependence on őł, but
the optimal network for a given schedule does depend on őł, because it approximates the score of the schedule-dependent
marginal pt(¬∑; őł) = p0 ‚ąóN(0, Mt(őł)). We therefore present schedule learning as the following bilevel problem:
Let L(őł, Ōē) be as defined in (11). Define
Ōē‚ąó(őł) ‚ąąarg min
Ōē L(őł, Ōē),
‚áĒ
H(őł)
:= L(őł, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) = min
Ōē L(őł, Ōē).
(15)
From (15), the joint minimization over (őł, Ōē) is equivalent to
min
őł,Ōē L(őł, Ōē) = min
őł
H(őł).
Lemma 3.1 implies that, in the infinite-data/capacity limit, net(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) = ‚ąáx log pt(x; őł) for any őł.
The challenge of computing ‚ąāőłH(őł):
When optimizing őł in (15), we need ‚ąāőłH(őł). Because H(őł) evaluates the loss
at the őł-dependent optimum Ōē‚ąó(őł), differentiating H involves computing the derivative of the optimum score field wrt
őł, i.e. ‚ąāőłnet(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) ‚Čą‚ąāőł‚ąálog pt(x, őł). Even though net(x, t, Ōē) does not depend on őł, an implicit dependency
on őł is introduced via Ōē‚ąó(őł).
The difficulty arises because at any point during training, we only observe the current value of Ōē. There is no
simple way to directly compute ‚ąāőłnet(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) or even ‚ąāőłŌē‚ąó(őł). However, there is remarkable structure in
pt(x, őł) = p0 ‚ąóN(0, Mt(őł)). By exploiting this fact, we show that ‚ąāőł‚ąálog pt(x, őł) can be written using only
derivatives wrt x, without explicit derivatives wrt őł. In turn, this enables us to approximate ‚ąāőłnet(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) using
only x-derivatives of the network.
4.1
A Plug-in Approximation for ‚ąāőł‚ąálog pt via Stochastic Calculus
Below we provide an identity for expressing the őł-derivative of the true score ‚ąāőł‚ąálog pt(x, őł) using terms that only
depend on entries of ‚ąá2 log pt(x, őł).
Theorem 4.1. Let őł ‚ąąRc and let j ‚ąą{1, . . . , c} be any coordinate. Then for all x ‚ąąRd,
‚ąāőłj‚ąálog pt(x; őł) = 1
2
d
X
i=1
‚ąār‚ąās ‚ąálog pt

x + rei + s ‚ąāőłjMt(őł) ei; őł

r=s=0
+ ‚ąās ‚ąálog pt

x + s ‚ąāőłjMt(őł) ‚ąálog pt(x; őł); őł

s=0.
(16)
Let Ōē‚ąó(őł) be defined as in (15). Under sufficient model capacity and data,
‚ąāőłjnet(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) = 1
2
d
X
i=1
‚ąār‚ąās net

x + rei + s ‚ąāőłjMt(őł) ei, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)

r=s=0
+ ‚ąās net

x + s ‚ąāőłjMt(őł) net(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)), t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)

s=0.
(17)
We defer the proof to Appendix B.2.
4.2
Flow Parameterization and Variance Reduction
A practical concern is that ‚ą•net(x, t, Ōē)‚ą•2 typically scales like ‚ą•Mt(őł)‚ąí1/2‚ą•2, which can vary significantly across noise
levels and inflate gradient variance. We therefore work with the normalized vector field
flow(x, t, Ōē) := Mt(őł)1/2 net(x, t, Ōē),
(18)
5


--- Page 6 ---
whose scale is approximately time-invariant. Using Theorem 4.1, we can also express ‚ąāőłflow in terms of x-directional
derivatives:
Corollary 4.2. Under the same setup as Theorem 4.1,
‚ąāőłflow(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) = 1
2
d
X
i=1
‚ąār‚ąās flow

x + rei + s ‚ąāőłMt(őł) ei, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)

r=s=0
+ ‚ąās flow

x + s Mt(őł)‚ąí1/2 ‚ąāőłMt(őł) flow(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)), t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)

s=0
+ 1
2 Mt(őł)‚ąí1␀‚ąāőłMt(őł)

flow(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)).
We defer the proof of Corollary 4.2 to Appendix B.3.
4.3
Final Optimization Formulation for H(őł) = minŌē L(őł, Ōē)
We now clarify how the schedule gradient uses (17) (or equivalently Corollary 4.2). We emphasize that the additional
‚ąāőłnet(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) term appears when differentiating the outer value function H(őł) = minŌē L(őł, Ōē), not when
differentiating L(őł, Ōē) with Ōē held fixed.
For notational clarity we treat őł as a scalar below; the vector case follows by applying the same derivation to each
coordinate őłj. Let x0 ‚ąľp0, ŌĶ ‚ąľN(0, I), sample t as in Section 3, and set xt := x0 + Mt(őł)1/2ŌĶ. Using the flow form
of the loss, we verify that L(őł, Ōē) of (11) is equivalent to
L(őł, Ōē) = Ex0,ŌĶ,t
Wt(őł)
␀flow(xt, t, Ōē) + ŌĶ

2
2

Wt(őł) := (I + Mt(őł))‚ąí1/2 ‚ąātMt(őł) Mt(őł)‚ąí1/2.
(19)
We define the convenient notation ‚Ąď(v, ŌĶ, W) := ‚ą•W(őł)(v+ŌĶ)‚ą•2
2, so that L(őł, Ōē) = Ex0,ŌĶ,t [‚Ąď(flow(xt, t, Ōē), ŌĶ, Wt(őł))].
Chain rule for the outer objective.
Define flow‚ąó
őł(x, t) := flow(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) and recall H(őł) = L(őł, Ōē‚ąó(őł)). Differ-
entiating gives
‚ąāőłH(őł) =
‚ąāőłL(őł, Ōē)

Ōē=Ōē‚ąó(őł)
|
{z
}
explicit dependence through Mt(őł)
+ Ex0,ŌĶ,t
hD
‚ąáv‚Ąď(flow(xt, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)), ŌĶ, Wt(őł)), ‚ąāőłflow(xt, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł))

{z
}
implicit dependence through the optimal score/flow field
,
(20)
The second term on the RHS above uses the (per-sample) gradient of ‚Ąď(v, ŌĶ, t) with respect to v given by ‚ąáflow‚Ąďt =
2 ňÜWt(őł)‚ä§ňÜWt(őł)
␀v + ŌĶ

, this term isolates the dependence of H(őł) on Ōē‚ąó(őł). We estimate ‚ąāőłflow‚ąó
őł(xt, t) using
Corollary 4.2 (equivalently, estimate ‚ąāőłnet‚ąó
őł using (17)). In practice, the expectation in (20) is approximated by
minibatch averages over samples (x(j)
0 , ŌĶ(j), t(j)), for j = 1...BatchSize.
5
Denoising Algorithms for Matrix Trajectories
This section describes sampling (denoising) given (i) a learned PSD matrix trajectory Mt(őł) and (ii) a trained network
flow(x, t, Ōē), where
flow(x, t, Ōē) ‚ČąMt(őł)1/2 ‚ąáx log pt(x; őł).
We focus on reverse-time ODE sampling, which is the inference method used throughout our experiments. We remark
that our method generalizes easily to the reverse-time SDE setting.
5.1
Reverse-time ODE in flow form and initialization
Using net(x, t, Ōē) = Mt(őł)‚ąí1/2flow(x, t, Ōē), and the learned ODE from (7), the reverse-time ODE is equivalent to the
time-reversal of the following:
d¬Įxt = ‚ąí1
2 ‚ąātMt(őł) Mt(őł)‚ąí1/2 flow(¬Įxt, t, Ōē) dt,
(21)
For initialization we sample őĺ ‚ąľN(0, I) and set
¬ĮxT := MT (őł)1/2őĺ,
(22)
with T chosen large enough that pT is close to a centered Gaussian.
6


--- Page 7 ---
Why the solver uses increments of M 1/2
t
.
In this section, we will assume that the matrices Mt(őł) share eigenvectors
and therefore commute with ‚ąātMt(őł). This assumption is not necessary, but helps significantly simplify the algebra. In
Section 6, we discuss numerous examples of families of Mt(őł) which satisfy this assumption. Under this commutation,
1
2 ‚ąātMt(őł) Mt(őł)‚ąí1/2 = ‚ąāt
␀Mt(őł)1/2
,
so (21) becomes
d¬Įxt = ‚ąí‚ąāt
␀Mt(őł)1/2
flow(¬Įxt, t, Ōē) dt.
(23)
This reveals that the natural step size for anisotropic denoising is the matrix increment ‚ąÜM 1/2
t
, directly generalizing
the scalar EDM viewpoint where Mt = ŌÉ(t)2I and ‚ąÜM 1/2
k
= (ŌÉk‚ąí1 ‚ąíŌÉk)I.
5.2
Reverse Euler discretization
Let K be the number of solver steps and choose a discretization grid
0 = t0 < t1 < ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ < tK = T.
(24)
Reverse-time integration proceeds from tK down to t0. For convenience, define
Uk := Mtk(őł)1/2,
‚ąÜUk := Uk ‚ąíUk‚ąí1
(k = 1, . . . , K).
(25)
A first-order explicit Euler discretization of the time-reversal of (23) yields
¬Įxtk‚ąí1 = ¬Įxtk + ‚ąÜUk flow(¬Įxtk, tk, Ōē),
k = 1, . . . , K.
(26)
We present the above Euler-Discretization scheme (26) mainly as a baseline derivation; in our experiments, second-order
Heun consistently dominates Euler in FID per NFE.
5.3
Heun‚Äôs second-order method for matrix trajectories
We now present the matrix-valued generalization of Heun‚Äôs second-order algorithm. We inherit the notation from
Section 5.2 above. Fix a secondary evaluation time ňÜtk ‚ąą[tk‚ąí1, tk] and denote ňÜUk := MňÜtk(őł)1/2. Let
uk := ‚ąíflow(¬Įxtk, tk, Ōē).
We form a predictor at ňÜtk by
ňÜxňÜtk = ¬Įxtk + ( ňÜUk ‚ąíUk) uk,
ňÜuk := ‚ąíflow(ňÜxňÜtk, ňÜtk, Ōē).
(27)
At any time s ‚ąą[tk‚ąí1, tk], we approximate the intermediate flow field at s by the affine model
ňúu(s) := uk + (U(s) ‚ąíUk) ( ňÜUk ‚ąíUk)‚ąí1 (ňÜuk ‚ąíuk),
U(s) := Ms(őł)1/2.
Substituting this approximation into (23) and integrating yields the closed-form second-order update
¬Įxtk‚ąí1 = ¬Įxtk + ‚ąÜUk uk + 1
2 (‚ąÜUk)2 ( ňÜUk ‚ąíUk)‚ąí1 (ňÜuk ‚ąíuk).
(28)
We defer the proof to Lemma A.3 in the appendix. When ňÜtk = tk‚ąí1 (endpoint choice), we have ňÜUk = Uk‚ąí1 and (28)
reduces to the familiar Heun form
¬Įxtk‚ąí1 = ¬Įxtk + ‚ąÜUk
uk + ňÜuk
2
,
which matches the standard scalar EDM/Heun update when Mt = ŌÉ(t)2I. In experiments we consider two choices of
ňÜtk, i.e. ňÜtk = tk‚ąí1 (endpoint) and ňÜtk = (tk‚ąí1 + tk)/2 (midpoint).
The solver grid {tk}K
k=0 is treated as an orthogonal design choice. In our experiments, for given total step K, we take
t1...tK to be a uniform grid over [0...T].
Efficient implementation under structured parameterizations.
All updates require only (i) matrix‚Äďvector products
with Mt(őł)1/2 and (ii) product of ( ňÜUk ‚ąíUk)‚ąí1 with vectors. Under the projector-based families in Section 6, these
operations reduce to inexpensive subspace-wise scalings (and subspace-wise pseudoinverses), avoiding explicit d √ó d
matrix square roots or dense linear algebra.
7


--- Page 8 ---
6
Practical Choices of Matrix Schedule Mt(őł)
The framework in Sections 3‚Äď5 applies to arbitrary monotone PSD matrix trajectories Mt(őł). Below, we demonstrate
a number of specific parametric families of Mt(őł). In each case, we verify that the parameterization satisfies: (i)
monotonicity (‚ąātMt(őł) ‚ČĽ0), (ii) efficient evaluation of matrix functions appearing in the loss and sampler (e.g.
M ¬Ī1/2
t
and (I + Mt)‚ąí1/2), and (iii) commutation of ‚ąātMt(őł) and Mt(őł).
6.1
A subspace-schedule framework
We use a broad family based on an orthogonal decomposition of Rd into subspaces. Let {Pj}J
j=1 be orthogonal
projection matrices such that Pj = P ‚ä§
j ‚™į0, P 2
j = Pj, PjPk = 0 for j Őł= k, and PJ
j=1 Pj = I. Let {gj(¬∑; őł)}J
j=1 be
nonnegative, nondecreasing scalar functions with gj(0; őł) = 0 and gj(T; őł) = T. In our experiments, each gj(¬∑; őł) is
parameterized by a monotone knot-based family (Appendix C), which ensures gj is nondecreasing and differentiable in
őł. We define the matrix-valued schedule
Mt(őł) :=
J
X
j=1
gj(t; őł) Pj,
(24)
so that ‚ąātMt(őł) = PJ
j=1 ňôgj(t; őł)Pj ‚™į0 whenever each gj is nondecreasing. This construction therefore satisfies the
monotonicity constraint (3) automatically. Because the Pj are mutually orthogonal projectors, any (scalar) matrix
function f acts subspace-wise:
f(Mt(őł)) =
J
X
j=1
f(gj(t; őł)) Pj.
(25)
In particular, the quantities needed by our loss and sampler‚ÄĒM ¬Ī1/2
t
, M ‚ąí1
t
, and (I + Mt)‚ąí1/2‚ÄĒreduce to inexpensive
scalings on each subspace. This also matches the solver structure in Section 5, where updates depend on increments of
M 1/2
t
; under (24), these increments are simply subspace-wise scalar increments.
The form of (24) separates:
‚ÄĘ Directions / subspaces (the projectors {Pj}, determined by a basis choice and a grouping), and
‚ÄĘ Time allocation (the scalar schedules {gj(¬∑; őł)}).
This separation lets us trade off interpretability and expressivity while keeping training and sampling efficient.
6.2
Schedule families used in experiments
We now list the concrete instances of (24) used in our experiments in Section 7.
(a) Isotropic schedule (warm-up / special case).
Standard isotropic diffusion corresponds to J = 1 with P1 = I, so
Mt(őł) = g(t; őł)I. This serves as a sanity check and warm-up baseline; it also emphasizes that our framework strictly
generalizes scalar schedule learning.
(b) Fixed structured basis (DCT subspaces).
For images, we often choose a fixed orthonormal transform U ‚ąąRd√ód
exposing interpretable directions (e.g. a 2D-DCT basis; see Appendix F.2). Partition the basis indices into J groups
defining subspaces S1, . . . , SJ (ordered by decreasing frequency), and let Pj be the projector onto Sj. A particularly
simple instance is J = 2: S1 is a low-frequency subspace and S2 = S‚ä•
1 its complement, giving
Mt(őł) = gDCT
1
(t; őł)P1 + gDCT
2
(t; őł)P2.
This parameterization yields an interpretable notion of anisotropy (coarse-to-fine denoising) while remaining cheap.
(c) Class-conditional PCA bases (data-dependent subspaces, shared schedules).
To incorporate data geometry, we
can make the basis depend on a class label y ‚ąą{1, . . . , C} (in the case of ImageNet and CIFAR10). For each class
y, compute a PCA basis from training samples in that class and form mutually orthogonal subspaces {S(y)
j
}J
j=1 that
partition Rd (e.g. S(y)
1
spans the top-k PCs and the remaining subspaces capture residual directions). Let P (y)
j
be the
projector onto S(y)
j
. We then define
Mt(őł; y) =
J
X
j=1
gPCA
j
(t; őł) P (y)
j
.
8


--- Page 9 ---
Here the subspaces vary with y while the scalar schedules are shared across classes, isolating the effect of a class-
dependent geometry.
(d) Shared structured basis + class-conditional schedules.
For class-conditional generation with a score network
net(x, t, y; Ōē), we can instead keep the projectors fixed (e.g. DCT projectors {Pj}) and make the schedules class-
specific:
Mt(őł; y) =
J
X
j=1
gDCT
j,y
(t; őł) Pj.
Even in the simple J = 2 case this yields 2C coupled scalar trajectories, making black-box search over őł impractical
and motivating our gradient-based optimization of őł.
(e) Class-conditional PCA bases + class-conditional schedules (most expressive).
Finally, we can let both the
subspaces and schedules depend on class:
Mt(őł; y) =
J
X
j=1
gPCA
j,y
(t; őł) P (y)
j
.
This is the largest design space we consider. It can align anisotropic diffusion dynamics with class-dependent data
geometry, but it also highlights why an efficient variational optimization scheme is needed.
Discussion: expressivity vs. efficiency.
Across (a)‚Äď(e), the template (24) ensures that all matrix operations required
by the trajectory loss and the anisotropic solvers reduce to subspace-wise scalings via (25). Increasing expressivity
corresponds to increasing J, allowing class dependence, or making the basis data-dependent (PCA), while retaining
tractable computation.
7
Experiments
We evaluate learned anisotropic diffusion schedules on four standard image generation benchmarks: CIFAR-10
(32 √ó 32) [18], AFHQv2 (64 √ó 64) [19], FFHQ (64 √ó 64) [20], and ImageNet-64 (64 √ó 64) [21]. All comparisons are
against the EDM baseline [3], using the official EDM implementation and its recommended hyperparameter settings for
each dataset/configuration.
Training protocol.
Our models are initialized from the corresponding pretrained EDM networks and then fine-tuned
while learning the schedule parameters őł (and continuing to update network parameters Ōē) as in (19). Across all datasets,
fine-tuning consumes the equivalent of 1.2M image passes (as measured by total images processed). See Appendix D
for full training details.
Evaluation protocol.
We generate 50k samples and compute Fr√©chet Inception Distance (FID‚Üď). For both EDM
and our method, we repeat sampling three times (independent random seeds) and report the minimum FID across the
three runs, matching the EDM evaluation protocol [3]. We report results across a range of solver budgets measured by
number of function evaluations (NFE), where one function evaluation corresponds to one forward pass of the score/flow
network during sampling. For Heun‚Äôs second-order solver, each step uses two network evaluations, and when the
secondary time is chosen as an endpoint (ňÜtk = tk‚ąí1) the final evaluation at tk‚ąí1 is reused as the first evaluation of
the next step. This yields the familiar odd NFE counts (approximately 2K ‚ąí1 for K solver steps), consistent with
EDM [3]. Unless otherwise noted, we use the Heun solver described in Section 5.
7.1
Schedule variants evaluated
We compare the following schedule families (see Section 6 for the corresponding parameterizations):
1. EDM. The original EDM baseline with isotropic schedule.
2. Learned isotropic schedule g. A single learned scalar schedule with Mt = g(t; őł)I.
3. Class-conditional isotropic schedules {gy}C
y=1. One scalar schedule per class: Mt(y) = gy(t; őł)I.
4. DCT anisotropic schedule (gDCT
1
, gDCT
2
). A two-subspace matrix schedule with J = 2 in a fixed 2D-DCT basis
(Appendix E), where the ‚Äúlow-frequency‚ÄĚ subspace consists of the (H/2)√ó(H/2) lowest-frequency DCT modes (i.e.,
H2/4 basis vectors) and the second subspace is its orthogonal complement: Mt = gDCT
1
(t; őł)P1 + gDCT
2
(t; őł)P2.
9


--- Page 10 ---
5. PCA anisotropic schedule (gPCA
1
, gPCA
2
). A two-subspace matrix schedule with J = 2 using class-conditional
PCA bases (subspaces depend on class, schedules are shared), as described in Section 6.
6. Class-conditional DCT anisotropic schedules {(gDCT
1,y , gDCT
2,y )}C
y=1. Fixed DCT subspaces but class-specific scalar
schedules.
7. Class-conditional PCA anisotropic schedules {(gPCA
1,y , gPCA
2,y )}C
y=1. The most expressive conditional variant we
consider: both PCA subspaces and schedules depend on class.
Conditional vs. unconditional datasets.
We consider CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-64 as class-conditional datasets. For
CIFAR-10, which is relatively simple, we first train isotropic schedules, including both a global isotropic schedule and
class-conditional isotropic schedules, as warm-up ablations. Building on this, we separately study the effects of class-
conditional schedules and class-conditional bases. For ImageNet-64, due to its higher visual complexity, we directly
adopt class-conditional isotropic schedules during warm-up. We then evaluate more expressive variants, including
class-conditional schedules, class-conditional bases, and their combination. For AFHQv2 and FFHQ, which do not
provide class labels, we primarily train structured-basis anisotropic schedules based on a fixed DCT decomposition
with a global isotropic schedule g as an ablation for warm-up.
CIFAR-10
Method

nfe 11
nfe 13
nfe 15
nfe 17
nfe 35
nfe 59

Table 1: FID ‚Üďvs. NFE across datasets (50k samples). See section 7 for description of each method. For each method
(both EDM and ours), we perform 3 independent random generations of 50k images and report the minimum FID
across the three runs. Bold = per-NFE best. Blue = Best FID lower than EDM.
7.2
Discussion of results
Table 1 reports FID across solver budgets (NFE). Figures 2‚Äď5 (Appendix E) visualize the learned schedules and
anisotropy ratios.
(i) Consistent FID improvement over EDM baseline.
At the best-performing NFE in Table 1, we observe consistent
improvements over EDM: CIFAR-10 improves from 1.829 (EDM) to 1.803 (PCA schedule); AFHQv2 improves
10


--- Page 11 ---
from 2.042 to 2.010 (DCT anisotropic); FFHQ improves from 2.374 to 2.242 (learned isotropic g); and ImageNet-64
improves from 2.276 to 2.238 (class-conditional DCT anisotropic). These gains persist across multiple NFE settings.
(iii) Matrix-valued anisotropy demonstrates benefits.
Beyond the isotropic warm-up, matrix-valued schedules are
often beneficial. On CIFAR-10 and AFHQv2, anisotropic variants improve upon EDM baseline or learned isotropic
schedules across a wide range of NFEs. On ImageNet-64, class-conditional anisotropic variants are the strongest, with
{(gDCT
1,y , gDCT
2,y )}C
y=1 achieving the best FID (2.238).
(iv) Class-conditional variants matter most on complex conditional data.
On both CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-64,
class-conditional variants outperform their global counterparts. On CIFAR-10, class-conditional bases (gPCA
1
, gPCA
2
)
achieve the best FID of 1.803. On ImageNet-64, class-conditional schedules {(gDCT
1,y , gDCT
2,y )}C
y=1 yield the best FID of
2.238. These results indicate that class-dependent schedule parameterizations are beneficial in practice.
8
Conclusion
We introduced a variational framework for learning anisotropic diffusion models by replacing the scalar noise schedule
with a monotone PSD, matrix-valued trajectory Mt(őł) that allocates noise (and denoising effort) across directions
and subspaces. Central to the framework is a trajectory-level score-matching objective that jointly trains the score
network and optimizes the schedule, and admits a natural pathwise change-of-measure interpretation: the loss controls
a tractable surrogate of the discrepancy between ideal and learned dynamics integrated along full trajectories. In the
isotropic special case, the formulation reduces to standard weighted score matching‚ÄĒa useful consistency check‚ÄĒbut
its main purpose is to enable principled optimization over general matrix-valued schedules.
To make trajectory optimization practical, we derived an efficient plug-in estimator for schedule gradients using
higher-order directional derivatives of the network, together with a flow parameterization that stabilizes scale across
noise levels and reduces variance. For inference, we developed anisotropic reverse-time ODE solvers by generalizing
Euler and second-order Heun updates to matrix trajectories, yielding closed-form steps expressed through increments of
M 1/2
t
that can be implemented efficiently under structured parameterizations.
Empirically, we observe consistent improvements over the baseline across a wide range of datasets and settings;
strongest performance was observed for complex class-conditional matrix schedules, underscoring the importance of a
efficient variational approach to selecting anisotropic trajectories.
Looking forward, it is promising to expand the space of efficiently-computable matrix trajectories beyond orthogonal
projector decompositions and to explore richer conditioning, as well as other modalities.

A
Additional Lemmas
Lemma A.1. If xt evolves as either one of the two processes:
1. (xt ‚ąíx0) ‚ąľN(0, Mt(őł))
2. dxt = ‚ąí1
2‚ąātMt(őł)‚ąálog pt(xt; őł)dt,
where pt(x; őł) := p0 ‚ąóN(0, Mt(őł)), then the score above is the conditional expectation
‚ąálog pt(x; őł) = M ‚ąí1
t
(őł)Ex0|xt=x [x0 ‚ąíxt] ,
where (x0, xt) are defined by the joint distribution x0 ‚ąľp0 and xt = x0 + N(0, Mt).
Proof of Lemma A.1. Fix t and őł, and write ő£ := Mt(őł). For notational simplicity we suppress the dependence on
(t, őł) when clear. The marginal pt(¬∑; őł) is the Gaussian convolution
pt(x; őł) =
Z
Rd p0(x0) ŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0) dx0,
ŌÜő£(z) := (2ŌÄ)‚ąíd/2|ő£|‚ąí1/2 exp

‚ąí1
2z‚ä§ő£‚ąí1z

.
Assuming ő£ ‚ČĽ0, we may differentiate under the integral sign to obtain
‚ąáxpt(x; őł) =
Z
p0(x0) ‚ąáxŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0) dx0.
Since ‚ąáxŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0) = ő£‚ąí1(x0 ‚ąíx) ŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0), we get
‚ąáxpt(x; őł) = ő£‚ąí1
Z
p0(x0) (x0 ‚ąíx) ŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0) dx0.
Dividing by pt(x; őł) yields
‚ąáx log pt(x; őł) = ő£‚ąí1
R
p0(x0) (x0 ‚ąíx) ŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0) dx0
R
p0(x0) ŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0) dx0
= ő£‚ąí1 E [x0 ‚ąíx | xt = x] ,
where the conditional distribution is p(x0 | xt = x) ‚ąĚp0(x0)ŌÜő£(x ‚ąíx0). Finally, since xt = x on the conditioning
event, x0 ‚ąíx = x0 ‚ąíxt, giving the stated identity.
Lemma A.2. For any w(t), there exists a gt(őł) and constant c, such that for any h(t)

Proof of Lemma 3.1

B.2
Proof of Theorem 4.1
To simplify notation, we will drop the index j and treat őł as a scalar. The general proof for őł ‚ąąRc follows by repeating
the proof for each őłj, while holding all other őł‚Ä≤
ks fixed. Recall that pt(¬∑; őł) = p0 ‚ąóN(0, Mt(őł)). By the the heat
equation (applied to ‚ąāőłMt(őł) instead of ‚ąātMt(őł)),
‚ąāőłpt(x; őł)
=1
2div(pt(x; őł)‚ąāőłMt(őł)‚ąálog pt(x; őł))
=1
2pt(x; őł)div(‚ąāőłMt(őł)‚ąálog pt(x; őł))
+ 1
2pt(x; őł) ‚ü®‚ąálog pt(x; őł), ‚ąāőłMt(őł)‚ąálog pt(x; őł)‚ü©.
Dividing both sides by pt(x; őł) gives
‚ąāőł log pt(x; őł)

This concludes the proof of (17). To prove (17), we combine (17) with the guarantee that net(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)) =
‚ąálog pt(x; őł) from Lemma 3.1.

‚ąāőłnet(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł))
=1
2
d
X
i=1
‚ąār‚ąāsnet(x + rei + s‚ąāőłMt(őł)ei, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł))
+ ‚ąāsnet(x + s‚ąāőłMt(őł)net(x, t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)), t, Ōē‚ąó(őł)).

--- Page 16 ---

‚ąāőłflow(x; Ōē‚ąó(őł))
=Mt(őł)1/2‚ąāőłnet(x; Ōē‚ąó(őł)) + 1

C
Implementation Details of gi
For a fixed set of node locations 0 = ŌĄ0 < ŌĄ1 < ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ < ŌĄK‚ąí1 = T, the subspace noise schedule is defined in
log‚Äďspace between the smallest and largest variance values g(0) = g0 and g(T) = T. The trainable parameters
őł = (őł1, . . . , őłK‚ąí1) define a collection of strictly positive increments
si = softplus(őłi),
i = 1, . . . , K ‚ąí1,
These increments are then rescaled by őĪ so that their sum exactly matches the total log‚Äďgap between the endpoints,
őĪ = log T ‚ąílog g0
PK‚ąí1
i=1 si
.
The log‚Äďvalues at the nodes are constructed by cumulative summation,
‚Ąď0 = log g0,
‚Ąďj = ‚Ąď0 +
j
X
i=1
őĪsi,
j = 1, . . . , K ‚ąí1,
so that ‚ĄďK‚ąí1 = log T.
Given a time t ‚ąą[0, T], one locates the enclosing interval [ŌĄj‚ąí1, ŌĄj] and computes the normalized position
p(t) = t ‚ąíŌĄj‚ąí1
ŌĄj ‚ąíŌĄj‚ąí1
.
The value of g(t) is then obtained by linearly interpolating between successive log‚Äďnodes and exponentiating:
log g(t) = (1 ‚ąíp(t))‚Ąďj‚ąí1 + p(t)‚Ąďj,
g(t) = exp
␀log g(t)

.
Within each interval the derivative takes the simple form
g‚Ä≤(t) = g(t)‚Ąďj ‚ąí‚Ąďj‚ąí1
ŌĄj ‚ąíŌĄj‚ąí1
.
D
Detailed Training Setup
Initialization.
All experiments are initialized from the corresponding pretrained EDM checkpoints. We load both raw
network weights and their exponential moving average (EMA) counterparts. When learning diffusion schedules, we
initialize schedule parameters from a pretrained isotropic schedule model. For conditional variants, schedule parameters
are instantiated per class. If class-wise isotropic schedules are available, they are used for initialization; otherwise, a
shared global schedule is copied across classes.
Joint optimization of network and schedule.
We jointly optimize network parameters Ōē and schedule parameters
őł using Adam (ő≤1 = 0.9, ő≤2 = 0.999, ŌĶ = 10‚ąí8). Network and schedule parameters use separate learning rates.
Gradients are accumulated over multiple micro-batches to achieve the target effective batch size. After backpropagation,
gradients are explicitly sanitized by replacing NaNs and infinities with finite values to ensure numerical stability.
For class-conditional schedules, schedule updates are applied only to classes present in the current batch; in distributed
settings, we synchronize the set of active classes across workers before applying optimizer steps to maintain consistent
parameter updates.
16


--- Page 17 ---
Learning-rate schedule.
We employ a linear warm-up for the first Kramp kimg. After warm-up, the learning rate
follows a mild decay schedule, with a decay factor applied after the midpoint of training. The learning rate of schedule
parameters scales proportionally to the network learning rate.
EMA update.
We maintain an exponential moving average of network parameters throughout training. The EMA
half-life is specified in kimg units. Optionally, we apply an EMA ramp-up, where the effective half-life is bounded by
the number of images seen so far during early training. EMA parameters are synchronized across workers in distributed
settings.
Distributed training.
For large-scale experiments (e.g., ImageNet-64), we use PyTorch Distributed Data Parallel
(DDP) with NCCL backend. The global batch is evenly partitioned across workers. All parameters are broadcast from
rank 0 at initialization. Gradients of network parameters are synchronized via DDP. Schedule parameters are explicitly
all-reduced across workers, including zero-filled gradients for classes absent on a given worker, to guarantee consistent
updates across GPUs.
Training budget and checkpoints.
Across all datasets, fine-tuning processes approximately 1.2M images in total. We
periodically save raw network weights, EMA weights, and schedule parameters. Unless otherwise specified, evaluation
is performed using the final EMA checkpoint.
E
Additional Experimental Analysis
Figures 2‚Äď5 visualize the learned schedules across datasets, subspace parameterizations, and conditioning schemes.
First, across all datasets (Figures 2‚Äď4), the learned anisotropic ratios deviate systematically from one over a substantial
portion of the diffusion horizon, confirming that the two subspaces are not treated symmetrically by the learned schedule.
However, the magnitude and shape of this deviation depend strongly on the dataset. For CIFAR-10 (Figure 2(b)) and
AFHQv2 (Figure 3), the anisotropy ratio exhibits a smooth mid-horizon amplification before returning to one near the
endpoints, suggesting that anisotropy is primarily active during intermediate diffusion times. In contrast, for FFHQ
(Figure 4) the ratio shows a more pronounced early amplification followed by a gradual decay, indicating stronger
low-frequency emphasis at early noise levels.
Second, on the class-conditional datasets (CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-64; Figures 2 and 5), class-conditional training
induces clear inter-class variability in both isotropic and anisotropic schedules. In CIFAR-10 (Figure 2(c,d)), the
class-conditional isotropic schedules display substantial dispersion around the geometric mean, and the anisotropic
schedules further amplify these differences. On ImageNet-64 (Figure 5(a‚Äďf)), this effect becomes more structured:
while class-conditional isotropic schedules vary moderately, the anisotropic variants reveal consistent class-dependent
shifts in both geometric means and anisotropy ratios. Notably, the PCA-based conditional schedules in Figure 5(d‚Äďf)
exhibit tighter concentration than their DCT counterparts (Figure 5(b,c)), suggesting that class-adaptive subspaces
reduce unnecessary variability.
F
Geometric Structure of Learned Subspaces
F.1
PCA Visualization
To analyze the geometry of the learned PCA subspaces, we first visualize the leading principal components for
representative classes (cat, dog, airplane) in Figure 6. Each row shows the top principal directions reshaped into spatial
patterns. We observe that semantically related classes (e.g., cat and dog) exhibit qualitatively similar dominant spatial
structures, whereas structurally distinct classes (e.g., airplane) show noticeably different patterns.
To quantify cross-class similarity, we compute pairwise distances between subspaces using the Frobenius norm of
projector differences, i.e.,
d (Q1, Q2) =
Q1Q‚ä§
1 ‚ąíQ2Q‚ä§
2

F
‚ąö
2k
,
where Q1, Q2 are orthonormal bases. We then apply classical multidimensional scaling (MDS) to embed this distance
matrix into two dimensions for visualization. MDS constructs a low-dimensional embedding whose pairwise Euclidean
distances best approximate the original subspace distances. Figure 7 reveals clear semantic clustering, with animal
classes grouped together and separated from vehicle classes.
These observations suggest that the learned PCA subspaces capture semantically meaningful geometric structure.
17


--- Page 18 ---

p

(t)gPCA
2
(t) with a log-linear

1
(t)/gPCA
2
(t). (c) Class-conditional isotropic
schedules gy(t)/¬Įg(t), where ¬Įg(t) =
␀ QC
y=1 gy(t)
1/C with C = #class = 10. (d) Class-conditional anisotropic
schedules over DCT subspaces: gDCT
k,y (t)/¬Įgk(t) for k ‚ąą{1, 2} (solid/dashed), where i indexes classes and ¬Įgk(t) is the
geometric mean across classes.

F.2
2D-DCT Transform Visualization

follows. For each pair (p, q) ‚ąą{0, . . . , H ‚ąí1}2, the basis is
ő¶p,q(x, y) = ő≥pő≥q cos

(2x+1)pŌÄ

Vectorizing each ő¶p,q into Rd and enumerating them yields the orthonormal basis {v1, . . . , vH2}, which are the
2D-DCT basis of Rd. Please refer to Figure 8 for example 2D-DCT bases.

Figure 6: Class-specific PCA subspaces. Leading principal components for representative CIFAR-10 classes (cat,
dog, airplane), reshaped as spatial patterns. Semantically related classes (e.g., cat and dog) exhibit qualitatively similar
dominant structures, whereas visually distinct classes (e.g., airplane) display different principal directions.
Figure 7: Subspace distance embedding via classical MDS. Pairwise distances between class-specific PCA subspaces
are computed using the Frobenius norm between orthogonal projectors. Classical multidimensional scaling (MDS)
embeds the resulting distance matrix into two dimensions. Animal classes cluster together and are separated from
vehicle classes, indicating that the learned subspaces capture semantically structured geometry.
